name: HDC-3A "Geysir"
description: The Geysir introduces plasma-torch thermal drilling — a confined argon plasma arc at the drill tip reaches 4,000 K, flash-sublimating ice-bearing regolith at contact rates far beyond what resistive or microwave heating can achieve. The extreme temperature differential creates a pressurized vapor column in the borehole that self-propels upward through the capture hood, eliminating the passive diffusion delay that limits extraction speed in lower-tier designs. A magnetic nozzle constrains the plasma arc to a narrow forward cone, preventing lateral borehole wall collapse. Drill depth extends to 20 metres, accessing deep ice deposits on Ceres-class bodies where surface desiccation layers can extend 10+ metres. At 16 tonnes the Geysir remains remarkably compact — the plasma source is essentially a modified electric arc thruster run in reverse — but the 18 MW power demand is substantial, requiring a high-output reactor-generator pair. Water output of 694 kg/hr approaches the throughput of equivalent-tier sifting plants at a fraction of the mass.
